name: Virginia

examFacts.notes: Administered by PSI for the Virginia Real Estate Board, part of the Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation (DPOR). The exam combines an 80-question national portion (105 minutes) and a 40-question Virginia state portion (45 minutes), both taken at a PSI testing center.

intro:
Virginia grades its two exam portions independently and holds the state half to a higher bar than the national side, so a strong national score won't carry you if your grasp of Virginia law is thin. The Virginia Real Estate Board licenses salespersons through the Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation, and Virginia law is woven through the state portion, so Virginia-specific preparation matters as much as the national principles every candidate studies.

Virginia runs on its own rules, including the Real Estate Board license law and regulations, designated and dual agency that require written consent, the buyer-beware Virginia Residential Property Disclosure Act, mandatory disclosures that range from the Chesapeake Bay Preservation Act to stigmatized property, trust and escrow account handling, and the property owners' association and condominium resale packets a seller has to deliver. The Virginia Fair Housing Law also reaches beyond the federal protected classes, down to elderliness and source of funds. None of that looks like the material a candidate in a neighboring state studies. A generic real estate practice test won't cut it. You need Virginia-specific practice questions.

faq.q: How many questions are on the Virginia real estate exam?
faq.a: The Virginia Real Estate Salesperson Examination has 120 scored multiple-choice questions, split into an 80-question national portion and a 40-question Virginia state portion. PSI also mixes in a handful of unscored experimental questions that look just like the real ones, and the time you spend on those does not count against you. The two portions are scored separately, so you have to pass both.

faq.q: What's the passing score for the Virginia real estate exam?
faq.a: You pass each portion on its own. The national portion takes 56 correct out of 80, which is 70 percent, and the Virginia state portion takes 30 correct out of 40, which is 75 percent. Because the two are scored independently, you can pass one and fail the other, and you only have to retake the portion you missed.

faq.q: How much does the Virginia real estate exam cost?
faq.a: The PSI examination fee is $60 per attempt, and it covers both the national and state portions. PSI holds your registration for one year and one exam, and the fee is not refundable or transferable, so it pays to be ready before you schedule. If you fail a portion you re-register and pay the exam fee again to retake it.

faq.q: How long is the Virginia real estate exam?
faq.a: PSI gives you 150 minutes of testing time, broken into 105 minutes for the 80-question national portion and 45 minutes for the 40-question Virginia portion. A short tutorial before the exam and the optional review at the end do not eat into that time. PSI runs Virginia test centers in Alexandria, Richmond, Roanoke, Virginia Beach, Norfolk, Fredericksburg, Harrisonburg, Vienna, and several other locations.

faq.q: Who administers the Virginia real estate exam?
faq.a: The Virginia Real Estate Salesperson Examination is administered by PSI for the Virginia Real Estate Board, part of the Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation (DPOR). You register and schedule your exam date through PSI.

faq.q: Is the Virginia real estate exam hard?
faq.a: The national portion is the same caliber you would find on any PSI real estate exam, and plenty of first-time candidates underestimate it. The Virginia state portion is shorter but unforgiving, since 40 questions leave little room for error at a 75 percent bar, and it draws straight from the Real Estate Board statutes and rules. Most people who struggle spent their study time on national principles and skimped on Virginia's agency, disclosure, and escrow rules. Honest practice on a Virginia-specific practice test is the difference between passing on the first try and paying the $60 exam fee a second time.

faq.q: What's on the Virginia real estate exam?
faq.a: The 80-question national portion covers property ownership, land use controls, valuation and market analysis, financing, agency, property disclosures, contracts, leasing and property management, transfer of title, the practice of real estate, and real estate math, in proportions set by PSI's content outline, with contracts and agency carrying the most weight. The 40-question Virginia portion covers license law and Real Estate Board rules, escrow and trust accounts, agency definitions and designated agency, Virginia disclosure requirements, the Virginia Fair Housing Law, and specific acts like the Condominium Act, the Residential Landlord and Tenant Act, and the Property Owners' Association Act.

faq.q: What's the best way to prepare for the Virginia real estate exam?
faq.a: After you finish your required pre-license coursework, the highest-leverage thing you can do is grind through practice questions with honest feedback on every miss. Generic real estate practice tests help with national principles, but they will not catch you on Virginia's agency rules, disclosure requirements, or the license law the state portion pulls from. The RealReady app gives you the full Virginia question bank plus the national bank, with progress tracking and a missed-question mode so you can drill what you are weak on. Most users study 30 to 60 minutes a day for two to four weeks before sitting the exam.

The 20 Virginia practice questions on this page are free, no account or signup needed. Unlocking the rest of the Virginia bank is a single in-app purchase you pay once and own, with no recurring charge and no time limit on access.

faq.q: Does RealReady cover the Virginia real estate exam?
faq.a: Yes. RealReady has a dedicated Virginia question bank built for the Virginia real estate exam, covering the national principles every candidate sees plus the Virginia law that trips people up, like the Real Estate Board license law, designated and dual agency requiring written consent, and the Virginia Residential Property Disclosure Act.

faq.q: Is RealReady a real estate license course?
faq.a: No. RealReady is exam practice, not a pre-license course, so it doesn't count toward Virginia's required pre-license education hours. Use it after or alongside your coursework to drill realistic practice questions and find your weak spots before exam day.
